(Reference Range-Negative). Imaging Chest xray IMPRESSION: Right middle lobe pneumonia with small bilateral pleural effusions. Patchy opacity in the retrocardiac region likely represents atelectasis CT Chest IMPRESSION: Atelectasis at the right middle lobe, with suggestion of narrowing at the right middle lobe bronchus; however, no definite mass seen on CT. Subtle opacities diffusely in the lung, mostly at the periphery, in the form of due to mild infective changes. Small nodule in the right upper lobe, ground-glass, measuring 3 mm. Soft tissue nodule or adherent mucus in the left main bronchus. Follow up CT or bronchoscopy is recommended. Complete collapse of vertebral T4 spine of uncertain age, correlate with point tenderness. Bilateral pleural effusions, more on the right. MRI and MRA of the head and neck IMPRESSION: Normal MRA of the head. The diminished flow signal seen in both middle cerebra artery branches on projection images appear artifactual as those branches are visualized on the source images. Normal MRA of the neck. There is no acute infarct seen on diffusion images. There is moderate brain atrophy seen. Periventricular hyperintensities indicate moderate changes of small vessel disease. There is a chronic left parietal cortical subcortical infarct identified. There is no midline shift or hydrocephalus. No evidence of acute or chronic blood products. Chest xray Heart size is mildly enlarged, unchanged but its appearance is exaggerated by the presence of low lung volumes. The right middle lobe atelectasis is redemonstrated. Note is also made of significant calcifications of the mitral annulus. There is small amount of bilateral pleural effusion. No pneumothorax is present. Right Upper Extremity Ultrasound IMPRESSION: No evidence of DVT. Antecubital fossa fluid collection; this may represent a joint effusion versus a fluid collection; it is in an atypical location for a bursal cyst. Further imaging is recommended to rule joint effusion. ECHO The left atrial volume is severely increased. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function are normal (LVEF>55%). Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The diameters of aorta at the sinus, ascending and arch levels are normal. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are moderately thickened. There is moderate aortic valve stenosis (valve area 1.0-1.2cm2). No aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. No masses or vegetations are seen on the mitral valve, but cannot be fully excluded due to suboptimal image quality. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. [Due to acoustic shadowing, the severity of mitral regurgitation may be significantly underestimated.] There is moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion. IMPRESSION: Moderate calcific aortic stenosis. Normal global and regional biventricular systolic function. Moderate pulmonary hypertension. MRI Right Arm Limited study as described above, demonstrating moderate-sized joint effusion. No bone erosion or articular destruction. This is a nonspecific finding. The possibility of an inflammatory or infectious process cannot be excluded. Nonspecific soft tissue edema. Doubt extra-articular abscess. Findings compatible with tendinosis of the flexor and extensor tendons.

The patient is a postmenopausal woman. She underwent an adrenalectomy in . Following the surgery, she began having spotting until about the end of when she began having heavy vaginal bleeding and was seen in the emergency department. A pelvic ultrasound revealed an enlarged fibroid uterus with a heterogeneous thickened endometrium measuring 14 mm. On she underwent an endometrial biopsy as well as removal of an endocervical polyp and Pap smear. Endometrial biopsy showed an endometrial adenocarcinoma, endometrioid type, FIGO grade 1, arising in the background of EIN. The endocervical polyp was benign. She was counseled regarding her diagnosis and the recommendation for definitive surgical management.

prominence, new from worrisome for lymphadenopathy. Correlation with chest CT advised. CT CHEST Extensive mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy is highly concerning for malignancy. Likely diagnoses are lymphoma, bronchogenic carcinoma originating in the right hilus or right lower lobe nodule, or metastatic, extrathoracic malignancy. The large subcarinal node may be the best target for bronchoscopic tissue sampling. Random micronodular and linear opacities, predominantly in the right upper lobe, due to lymphatic involvement with tumor, or lymphostasis due to central adenopathy. CXR Single AP upright portable view of the chest was obtained. Again, there is mediastinal and right hilar enlargement in this patient with mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy seen on CT from . No focal consolidation is seen. There is no large pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Cardiac silhouette is stable. CT CHEST Occlusion of the right upper lobe pulmonary artery and attenuation of the RLL superior segment artery and left main pulmonary artery by mass effect from the extensive mediastinal and hilar adenopathy. No evidence of infarct. Mediastinal and hilar adenopathy along with perisplenic and perihepatic adenopathy appears mildly increased from prior with stranding of surrounding fat. Unchanged 11mm right lower lobe nodule from recent prior. CXR Slight progression of mild pulmonary edema with persistent left basal atelectasis. Asymmetric opacification of the left lung of unclear etiology with density similar to layering effusion. Conventional lateral views may be helpful if the patient can tolerate transport. Path Cell block 19 gauge TBNA #7 C13-15404B TBNA 7, cell block: Positive for malignant cells. Consistent with small cell carcinoma. See note. Note: The tumor cells are immunoreactive for keratin AE1/AE3, CAM 5.2 with some cells showing dot like cytoplasmic staining. They are also immunoreactive for chromogranin , synaptophysin and TTF1. Some of the tumor cells are focally positive for p63. There is no immunoreactivity for CK 7, CK 20, MNF116 or LCA. The focal immunoreactivity for p63 raises the possibility of an additional component of squamous cell carcinoma. Clinical correlation is recommended. Path SPECIMEN: BONE MARROW ASPIRATE AND CORE BIOPSY DIAGNOSIS: HYPERCELLULAR BONE MARROW WITH METASTATIC CARCINOMA. SEE NOTE. Note: The metastatic tumor cells are immunoreactive for chromogranin and TTF-1. Although they are mostly non-immunoreactive for cytokeratin cocktail (AE1/AE3/CAM5.2), and synaptophysin, antigenic loss due to decalcification cannot be excluded. They do not express other lymphoid (CD20 CD3-), stem cell (CD34-) or myeloid (CD117-) markers. See separate pathology report for further characterization. MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION: Peripheral blood smear: The smear is adequate for evaluation. Erythrocytes are normochromic with slight anisopoikilocytosis including rare echinocytes. The white blood count is normal. Large atypical lymphocytes with prominent nucleoli are occasionally seen. The platelet count appears decreased; occasional large platelets are seen. A 200 cell differential shows 44% neutrophils, 1% bands, 36% lymphocytes, 10% monocytes, 3% eosinophils, 0%basophil, 4% metamyelocytes and 2% myelocytes. Aspirate smear: The aspirate material is inadequate for evaluation due to lack of spicules and hemodilution. A limited 200 cell differential shows 0% blasts, 0% promyelocytes, 1% myelocytes, 1% metamyelocytes, 61% bands/neutrophils, 0% eosinophils, 0% erythroids, 34% lymphocytes, 0% plasma cells, 3% monocytes. Clot section and biopsy slides: The core biopsy material is adequate for evaluation. It consists of two fragments measuring overall 1.0 cm core biopsy of trabecular marrow, cortical bone and periosteum with a cellularity of 60-80%. There is an interstitial and intravascular infiltrate of highly atypical cells occurring in clusters occupying 50% of overall cellularity. There is prominent mitosis and apoptosis in this area. In the remaining cellularity, M: E ratio estimate is normal. Erythroid precursors are normal in number and have mildly dyspoietic maturation, including cells with satellite nuclei. Myeloid precursors are normal in number with normal maturation. Megakaryocytes are normal in number with focal loose clustering and include occasional small hypolobated forms. Path Bone Marrow DIAGNOSIS: FLOW CYTOMETRY REPORT FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING The following tests (antibodies) were performed: 7, Kappa, Lambda, and CD antigens 2,3,5,7,10,19,20,23 and RESULTS: Three color gating is performed (light scatter vs. CD45) to optimize lymphocyte yield. Lymphocytes comprise ~25% of total analyzed events. Of these, a subset (~5% of total) have lower CD45 expression. Gating on this region demonstrates that these express pan B-cell markers CD19 (dim) along with CD20 (dim). They additionally appear to express CD5 (dim) as well as CD23. They do not express any other characteristic antigens including CD10 or FMC7. The clonality of these B-cells could not be reliably assessed by surface immunoglobulin expression, due to cytophilic staining patterns (i.e. non-specific binding of immunoglobulin light chain antibodies), a pattern which persists despite repeat washings. In the CD45-bright region, there is a smaller population of admixed B-cells with the normal bright CD20 expression. T cells comprise 66% of all lymphoid events and express mature lineage antigens. INTERPRETATION Immunophenotypic findings show the presence of an abnormal B-cell population with co-expression of CD5 and CD23 suspicious for involvement by a B cell lymphoproliferative disorder. The presence of cytophilic antibody precludes determination of clonality. However the immunophenotype (CLL-like) is suggestive of monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis. Please correlate with clinical, morphologic and other ancillary findings. Note: This test was performed using analyte specific reagents (ASRs). These ASRs have not been cleared or approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). However, the FDA has determined that such clearance or approval is not necessary This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by the Flow Cytometry Laboratory at which is licensed by to perform high complexity tests. This test was used for clinical purposes; it should not be regarded as for research. Path SPECIMEN SUBMITTED: immunophenotyping - EBUS/TBNA, 7 DIAGNOSIS: FLOW CYTOMETRY REPORT FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING The following tests (antibodies) were performed: 7, Kappa, Lambda, and CD antigens 2,3,7,8,10,19,20 and RESULTS: Three color gating is performed (light scatter vs. CD45) to optimize lymphocyte yield. CD45-bright, low side scatter gated lymphocytes are extremely scant. A majority of the analyzed events are in the CD45-dim, variable side-scatter region. INTERPRETATION Non-diagnostic study. Cell marker analysis was attempted, but was non-diagnostic in this case due to insufficient number of lymphoid cells for analysis. Review of corresponding cytospin preparations from material submitted to flow cytometry shows marked bacterial overgrowth and background clusters of atypical cells in a hemorrhagic background. Correlation with clinical findings and morphology (see separate pathology report) is recommended. Flow cytometry immunophenotyping may not detect all abnormal populations due to topography, sampling or artifacts of sample preparation. IMAGING: ======== MRI HEAD IMPRESSION: Gyriform pattern of slow diffusion in the frontoparietal lobes near the vertex could reflect cerebritis, particularly given the underlying subcortical signal abnormality seen on T2 and FLAIR images, and T2/FLAIR signal abnormalities in the occipital lobes. The differential diagnosis also includes a posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Multiple foci of susceptibility signal in both cerebellar hemispheres, along the occipital lobes, and along the frontal lobes near the vertex probably reflect hemosiderin, and could potentially reflect hemorrhagic metastases although the postcontrast images are severely limited due to patient motion. Repeating contrast-enhanced MRI images of the brain when the patient is able to be still would be helpful in further characterization of these lesions. Numerous lesions throughout the calvarium, clivus, and imaged upper cervical spine likely reflect osseous metastases.

This patient is a year old female with history of DMII, HTN, presenting from with a history of syncopal event on the day of admission. On the morning of admission, patient reports elevated BS to high 200s, for which she took her morning dose of lantus and 6U humalog. Upon re-check, her BS was She then ate a meal with carbohydrates. Later in the day, the patient reports walking around the mall with a friend when she suddenly felt lightheaded with blurry vision, a sensation she associates with hypoglycemia in the past. She took 3 glucose tablets. She then lost consciousness and fell to the floor, striking her head. Patient denied any chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, unilateral weakness/numbess, seizure-like activity prior to the incident. She presented to where CT Head demonstrated subarachnoid hemorrhage of the left frontal region without midline shift. She was neurologically intact at the OSH, and subsequently loaded with Dilantin. CT C-spine did not show any acute fractures or dislocations. Chest x-ray was unremarkable. Patient was sent here for neurosurgery evaluation. In the ED, initial VS: 99.3 72 122/58 16 97%RA. EKG showed normal sinus rhythm with rate Labs were significant for HCT 35.5 and Cr 1.3. Neurology was consulted who felt that patient was neurologically intact, and requested a repeat head CT in AM. Patient was given 1L NS. She has 20G x 2 left arm. At the time of transfer, VS: 110/63, 72 nsr, rr 16, sao2 99, temp 97.8. Currently, the patient reports no pain, discomfort, or other symptoms. States that she has never had fainting spells before except for once in childhood. Never had any chest pain, palpitations or shortness of breath. No nausea or vomiting, no diarrhea or constipation. Pt was not incontinent of urine or stool. She was conscious during the entire episode and felt it coming on as well as her head strike. She was not confused afterwards. She has never had any cardiac issues or history of cardiac disease. ROS: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, headache, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria.

She reports that she has periodically been slurring her words, and occasionally has trouble coming up with the appropriate word. She all notes that she has binocular blurring of her eyes, without any visual loss. She notes that the cramps in her legs are getting worse, as well as the painful pins and needles sensations in her lower extremities. She reports that at baseline she has issues with controlling her urination, but lately has been having more frequent issues with loss of urine, and not knowing when she needs to void. She denies any fecal incontinence. Finally she notes that her gait has become significantly more unsteady, and she is having difficulty ambulating with her walker. She notes that this constellation of symptoms is frequently present during her usual MS flares. She suspected that another flare was coming on, but tried to hold off until she saw her PCP . She was seen in the office today, and it was recommended that she be sent to the ED for evaluation and possible treatment of a presumed MS flare. On neuro ROS, the pt denies headache, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus or hearing difficulty. On general review of systems, the pt denies recent fever or chills. No night sweats or recent weight loss or gain. Denies cough, shortness of breath. Denies chest pain or tightness, palpitations. Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation or abdominal pain. No recent change in bowel or bladder habits. No dysuria. Denies arthralgias or myalgias. Denies rash. MS Dr. was diagnosed with MS in when she developed neurological symptoms of decreased vision in her left eye, bilateral leg numbness and problems walking. This happened 6 weeks after giving birth to her third child. Since this first relapse, she has experienced "flares" yearly with increased bladder symptoms and difficulties with walking. Also occasional bilateral blurred vision. The majority of these episodes described in the OMR seems to be related to UTIs. Continuous progression of symptoms since at least . recently admitted to the with one of her flares in . Treated with Cosyntropin, since Solu-Medrol causes psychosis, and antibiotics for UTI. She tried once in but as she had worsening of her symptoms at that time, has not tried it since. Solu-medrol for flares causes acute psychosis, but she responds well to Cosyntropin. Last hospitalization for MS flare was in due to symptoms of gait imbalance, bilateral leg numbness, dysarthria, and intermittent blurry vision with urinary incontinence.

No recent antibiotic use, sick contacts or travel. She initially presented to in with RLQ pain associated with bilious emesis and decreased PO intake. CT imaging confirmed a jejunal-jejunal intussusception and she was initially conservatively managed with bowel rest. Diagnostic laparoscopy was later performed given a recurrence of her pain symptoms. This demonstrated a 12-inch segment of inflammation without other abnormalities. She has had recurrent admissions for abdominal pain since then, with MR enterography demonstrating jejunal inflammation without ongoing intussusception, and small bowel enteroscopy noted villous atrophy concerning for celiac enteropathy, despite normal antibody testing. It has been postulated that she has intermittent recurrence of intussusception, in addition to celiac enteropathy and constipation concerns which cause her pain. She is followed by Dr. . In the ED, initial VS 98.8 74 137/87 16 99% RA. Laboratory data notable for WBC 13.8 with neutrophilia and no bandemia. Creatinine 0.7. LFTs reassuring. Lactate 1.2. U/A and UCG negative. INR 1.0. Abdominal imaging without acute findings. ACS surgery and GI consulted. She received Morphine 5 mg IV x 1, Zofran 4 mg IV x 1, Dilaudid 1 mg IV x 4 and 2L of normal saline. On arrival to the floor, her pain is moderate and she appears stable.

She did not see any asymmetry in his face, he was moving all extremities, and his speech was not slurred. She was concerned for hypoglycemia and gave him mango juice and sugar packets. When he woke up the next morning he noted a throbbing/pulsating right-sided headache that was predominantly temporal but involved the whole right side of his head. The pain was constant non-radiating, with no associated N/V or photo/phonophobia. Position change or straining did not make it worse. There has been associated tearing of both eyes since onset. Symptoms were constant over the weekend, fluctuating in severity from 4 out of 10 to 8 out of 10 at maximum. He had difficulty sleeping evening due to worsening headache pain and then awoke morning with new right eye ptosis and blurry vision. This is described as horizontal diplopia worse in left gaze, and vertical diplopia in both down and upgaze, with clear vision in rightward gaze. He measured hi blood pressure after waking up and noted it was 150s-160s systolic, which is higher than his usual. He first presented to where exam was notable for a right nerve palsy with ptosis and right pupil dilation. He had a NCHCT that was normal and was urgently transferred to for further neurologic evaluation. Of note, he has a history one other previous episode that was very similar in presentation years ago. He had discomfort followed by a right-sided throbbing and ptosis of the right eye. He went to urgent care for evaluation and BP was reportedly >200s systolic. A head CT was done at that time and was normal. On exam his pupils were noted to be asymmetric for the first time with the right pupil a few millimeters larger. This patient presented after being transferred from an outside hospital following large stab wounds to the right neck and anterior chest, followed by jumping out a window of 2 stories height. She was evaluated at an outside hospital with neck and chest CT's as well as lower extremity plain films and then sent via medflight for further management. Throughout her entire course at the outside hospital and in the trauma bay at she has been hemodynamically stable. CT angiogram of the neck revealed no injuries to the great vessels of the neck or chest. Findings on exam were notable for a 16cm knife wound to the right neck with lateral violation of the platysma and exposure of the intact external jugular vein. Imaging of the anterior chest revealed a tiny dot of air of uncertain etiology in the anterior mediastinum. Exam in the trauma bay was notable for a 1cm superficial appearing wound overlying the manubrium. Given the magnitude of the wounds and the close proximity to the external jugular vein on the neck and the air in the mediastinum, we took this patient to the operating room for exploration of both wounds. Consent was obtained with the assistance of a translator for both anesthesia and the surgical procedure. The patient understands the risks and benefits and wished to proceed with surgery.

The patient is a year-old right handed man with history of alcohol use disorder, longstanding smoker who presents with a 2 week history of episodic confusion, gustatory hallucinations, and left sided headaches. History provided by patient and friend at bedside. The patient was in his usual state of health until approximately 2 weeks ago, when he began developing a number of symptoms, including: -Severe headaches, located behind the left eye and radiating into left temporal and left posterior area of the head. Throbbing in quality. Not positional, not associated with Valsalva. No associated nausea, photophobia or phonophobia. Not relieved with over-the-counter ibuprofen, which patient has been taking quite frequently, several times a day without relief. -Episodes of confusion and loss of awareness. These are often preceded by an unusual taste in the mouth, which patient describes as "rancid and metallic." When he experiences this sensation, he knows that an episode is approaching. He then loses awareness of his surroundings. Per his friend, who has witnessed number of these episodes, patient then paces around the room, talking to himself. This lasts for about a minute, 2 minutes at the most. Afterwards, the patient's friend reports he appears "drained" and will either sit on the chair or the couch to relax. The patient is only able to note that he is confused afterwards, unknown how long this lasts. He has a difficult time assessing when these episodes began and end. Frequency is estimated to be times per day. -Word finding difficulties and memory issues. Per patient's friend, he has been "very fuzzy" for the last few weeks to 1 month. He notes that during conversations, he often has difficulties coming up with the correct word. Denies difficulties producing or comprehending speech. For example, he was unable to remember the name of his friend's partner, who he knows very well and sees often. During a conversation he will often ask the same question repeatedly within a 5 minute period. His friend notes that the patient has at times had more mild word finding difficulties over the last years, but nothing close to this point. There was one particular episode 1 week prior to presentation, in which the patient was having a conversation with his girlfriend, when he lost consciousness, fell to the ground, and bit his tongue. No shaking movements, no urinary incontinence. His girlfriend was eventually able to walk into the bedroom. The patient has no recollection of this event whatsoever, except that he woke up in bed feeling confused. Prior to developing the above symptoms, the patient was high functioning and independent. He works as the of a . The patient does report having intermittent night sweats for the last few years. He also believes he has had recent weight loss and has been having decreased oral intake for the last few weeks. No fevers or recent infection. Of note: -The patient has a long-standing history of alcohol use disorder. He drinks roughly shots of vodka per day ounces per day) but his last drink was 1 month ago. -The patient has had a long-standing history of GI complaints, particularly alternating diarrhea and constipation, for the last years. He reports that the constipation is at times so severe that he takes apple cider enemas and over-the-counter enemas. He is also tried a number of home remedies. He was scheduled to have a GI appointment for further assessment but did not show up to the appointment. His last colonoscopy was years ago. Her eyes were open and deviated upwards and then she began shaking all over (described as low amplitude, unclear if jerking or not) so he grabbed her and lowered her to the ground. They walked to the court house and she then had a similar episode. At this time, her husband alerted the police and they called EMS. This episode also self resolved, and she recognized her husband immediately afterwards. She was taken to an OSH where she had a repeat event, and then because she was desaturating they intubated her. She received. She received 10 mg Valium, followed by 20 mg Valium, and 1 g of Keppra, 100mg lidocaine IV, magnesium, ativan, and ketamine. She had a similar episode years ago after overdosing on gabapentin. While she was in the emergency department she experienced one episode of vomiting and dizziness when she got out of bed.

In brief this is an year-old woman with h/o diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (s/p chemo and XRT in to L clavicle, now in remission), atrial fibrillation (on coumadin), mild sCHF on echo (EF 45%, focal wall motion abnormality), hypertension, presents with 2 weeks of exertional dyspnea. She started having progressive SOB about 2 weeks ago and her breathing has been gradually worsening, to the point that she could not walk comfortably to her bathroom from her bedroom at home. She also noted increasing 5-pillow orthopnea. She went to her PCP for evaluation. At that time, she was noted to have a weight of 188LB (recorded as 194LB in was breathing comfortably on room air with a sat of 98% (no ambulatory sat recorded), and per note her lungs were clear to auscultation. Labs at that time showed BNP of 1505, creatinine of 1.0. CXR showed new small bilateral pleural effusions and small lung volumes without overt pulmonary vascular congestion. Patient returned home and the PCP planned to discuss starting PO furosemide with outpatient cardiologist. Patient's granddaugther and HCP called in on to the 's office to report patient with increasing orthopnea, wheezing, unable to lay down, and lower extremity edema L>R, so she was referred to the ED. In the ED initial vitals were: 97.2 64 119/77 18 94%. Labs showed Creatinine 1.5 (baseline 1.0), otherwise normal chem 7, normal CBC, BNP 579, lactate 2.3, INR therapeutic at 2.9. His symptoms resolved until night, when he again developed abdominal cramping and then noticed associated chest pressure. He was seen in follow up on reporting early satiety, anorexia and an unintentional weight loss of pounds over the last year, most in the past 6 weeks. He has had nausea and vomited on one occasion. After eating he noted abdominal bloating. He has had no GI or GU bleeding. He reports that his stools are of normal caliber. He had labs at on which showed a hemoglobin of 10.8, normal WBC, MCV 61.8, platelets of 416,000. Alk phos was elevated at Ferritin was 116, ESR 63 and TSH was normal. He was admitted to the from for an expedited work up and I was asked to see him on . During his hospital stay he had a CXR: Low lung volumes. No acute cardiopulmonary process, an abdomen/Pelvic CT scan which showed findings concerning for a heterogeneous soft tissue cecal mass measuring up to 6.7 x 4.5 cm, concerning for primary colonic malignancy. A follow-up colonoscopy is recommended for further assessment. No evidence of bowel obstruction. Multiple large new hypoattenuating hepatic lesions highly concerning for metastatic disease throughout both lobes of the liver. The largest hepatic lesion occupies most of the right hepatic lobe and measures 16.9 x 11.5 cm in the axial plane. The largest hypoattenuating lesion at the left hepatic lobe measures 6.3 x 5.1 cm in the axial plane. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation. No evidence of mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Chest CT with right chest wall lesion involving the right anterior costal pleura, anterior aspect of the right third rib and extending through the rib into the chest wall measuring 46 x 22 mm in the axial plane. No obvious overlying pectoralis muscle involvement but this cannot be excluded with certainty. No pleural effusion. 3 mm pulmonary nodule in the superior segment of the right lower lobe is new compared to prior. Mild centrilobular pulmonary emphysema. Centrilobular ground-glass opacities with an upper lobe predominance may represent respiratory bronchiolitis in the correct clinical setting. Nonsuspicious 2 mm nodule in the right upper lobe. Mild, but diffuse cylindrical bronchiectasis without bronchial wall thickening or impaction is nonspecific. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. He was started on mirtazapine for appetite. He was noted to have an elevated INR of 1.8 and received one unit of FFP and vitamin K prior to his biopsy on . The biopsy showed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with colonic origin. By IHC the tumor is positive for CK20, CDX-2 and negative for CK7 supporting this diagnosis. He had a single lumen Portacath placed by on . Tumor testing showed intact MMR (intact MLH-1, PSM-2, MSH-2 and MSH-6). Mr is a year old caucasian man with PMH of MDD with psychotic features, alcohol abuse, TLE, HIV, and no prior psychiatric hospitalizations or suicide attempts who self-presented to the ED yesterday evening with increased anxiety, depression, and a sensation that someone was following him and telling hm to hurt himself. He has been feeling increasingly depressed for the past four months, feeling rejected, that life is pointless, and with thoughts of giving up. He denies any SA but a few months ago did research on the internet on how to kill himself. He says over the past few weeks he has had an increased sensation that someone is following him and telling him to harm himself. He says this is neither an auditory or visual hallucination, but rather an 'incredibly strong feeling' of being followed. He denies past experiences where someone or something has commanded him to hurt himself, however, he reports prior episodes of feeling that something or someone was following him and seeing shadows out of the corner of his eye. He says these episodes were within the past years, usually during times of medication changes. He denies thoughts of hurting himself, and reports his presentation to the hospital was because he does not want to hurt himself and wants to stay safe. He feels safe on the unit and has no thoughts of harming himself or others. Mr. has been compliant with all of his medications and reports his last medication change was when his psychiatrist Dr. him on Invega 6 mg QHS on due to similar delusional experiences, which he has been told may be related to his epilepsy. He has reportedly not been using substances other than alcohol for the past few months, and says his alcohol use has been greatly reduced in the past four months to a few beers a months. He reports his last drink was when he had approximately 4 drinks. Psych ROS positive for anhedonia, decreased sleep, decreased appetite, psychomotor agitation, decreased energy, and depressed mood as well as increased anxiety, avoidance of people and situations. He denies symptoms of mania. He denies AH/VH. He does report olfactory hallucinations and attributes these to seizure aura. He endorses the paranoid delusion described above. Course in Emergency Department : Patient was anxious/distressed in the ED and wanted to leave. He was given 1 mg ativan which helped to calm him. He maintained good behavioral control.

CORONARY ARTERIOGRAPHY AND PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION Coronary angiography: right dominant LMCA: Normal LAD: There was a 50% stenosis in the mid LAD with a large thrombus consistent with a proximal ulcerated plaque. There was a medium sized diagonal branch. The mid and distal LAD were free of significant obstructive disease. LCX: The LCx was a large vessel. There was a large ramus branch without disease. The LCx terminated in a large left posterolateral and a smaller left posterolateral branch. RCA: There was a 30% stenoses in the mid RCA. Interventional details There patient had typical symptoms of unstable angina and borderline EKG changes in the anterior leads. The troponins were negative. Unfractionated heparin and intravenous eptifibatide - double bolus - were used for anticoagulation. The LAD thrombus was deemed the culprit lesion. The LAD was crossed with a 0.014 wire and aspiration thrombectomy was performed x 2 removing white thrombus and plaque. There was modest improvement of the angiographic appearance of the lesion. A Spider filter wire was placed into the mid-distal LAD prior to stent placement. A 4.0 mm x 12 mm Promus Element stent was placed in the proximal LAD - but "watermelon seeded" during deployment to the central aorta. In order to deploy the stent in a suitable artery, the stent was withdrawn into the mid portion of the right radial artery and was fully deployed. The right groin was accessed and repeat angiography showed no residual thrombus and a 30% stenosis of the proximal LAD. As a result, no additional intervention was performed. The patient will be managed with IV eptifibatide x 18 hours, aspirin, and prasugrel. The stent deployment location was explained to the patient and his wife. surgery consultation was obtained and recommended conservative therapy. G1P1 presents with several weeks of worsening RLQ abdominal pain. Initially she noted that the pain was intermittent and not present for all of the day. She noticed around that time an increase in fluctuating episodes of diarrhea and constipation but did not find that the pain changed with these. She presented for evaluation at an OSH where a CT was reportedly negative for appendicitis. The pain continued to be intermittent. She was seen by her usual GI (Dr. on and he thought that it was possibly her IBS; testing was negative for celiac disease. Over the weekend, her pain worsened and became more constant with radiation to the lower back/flank. The pain did not change with bowel movements. She has felt nauseous and had a decreased appetite, but she believes that this is secondary to the pain. She denies actually vomiting. She had a bowel movement last 3 days ago and has felt like she has had "things moving in there" today, although doesn't note recent flatus. She has had occasional chills with hot flashes but no outright fevers. She denies chest pain or shortness of breath. She denies dysuria. She has taken tylenol/motrin at home for the pain but stopped as they weren't helping her. She has attempted intercourse recently, but had to halt due to intense worsening of the pain with deep penetration. She has had nephrolithiasis in the past and this does not feel similar. Notably, she had a laparoscopic hysterectomy in . This was complicated by the finding of a vaginal cuff dehiscence with repair in . She presented to on am with this worsened pain. A CT was done and read as likely SBO. The patient was transferred to BI for evaluation by surgery. She received morphine for her pain.

w gout, AVNRT s/p ablation, former smoker, HTN presents with abdominal pain and swelling. Patient was in USOH until about weeks when he started to have mild diffuse abdominal pain, which progressed to RUQ and LLQ pain over the last few days. He was just on a sailing trip in . While he was in he went to a hospital and was admitted for a few days. He had a RUQUS which showed ascites, CT abdomen which showed a mass in pancreas which he was told was benign, reportedly an MRI (which was reportedly reassuring regarding the mass) and was discharged on flagyl for a possible parasitic infection along with a PPI and PRN APAP. He returned home on day prior to admission, and presented to the ED to expedite workup. He reports lbs weight loss a few weeks ago, but then over the past couple weeks he has gained about 5lbs despite anorexia. He is not sure if he had sweats/night sweats as he was in a very warm climate, but his girlfriend says that the compartment on the sailboat was air conditioned and he was still sweating. On questioning, he reports that he had a high subjective fever 2d ago, but did not have a thermometer. He reports the abdominal pain is worst now in RUQ and associated with abdominal distention and gas. He also has pain in LLQ. He has not been eating much. Of note, patient has had R shoulder pain x months. It is not severe. It is aching, he saw his PCP for it before he left for his trip and it was thought to be musculoskeletal. It is worse with activity but not with any particular motion. There is no trauma. There is no swelling/erythema. It is not exertional and not associated with chest pain or shortness of breath. It is not limited in motion. No FHx cancer, though family history is limited as below. On questioning, he endorses chest pressure yesterday, not pain, + sub-sternal, while he was in the airport, not sudden, not pleuritic, not sure if it was exertional as he didn't exert himself much. Not reproducible. No trauma, no history of clots. No chest wall trauma. Resolved spontaneously, does not have anymore. No shortness of breath. No cough. Reports mild strange sensation when urinating as well as baseline urinary urgency. No hematuria, but urine has been darker at times. No diarrhea, no melena, no hematemesis or vomiting but has felt nauseous at times. No joint pain, no myalgias, no rash. No palpitations, orthopnea, PND. This is a year-old gentleman with long-standing history of stricturing Crohn's disease status post multiple small bowel resections including two at an outside hospital prior to an ileocecectomy in with subsequent revision of bayonet anastomosis one year ago in the setting of small bowel obstruction, now presenting with abdominal pain. He endorses gradual onset periumbilical pain that started approximately 12 hours prior to presentation to an outside hospital, first sharp in quality, later described as a "drilling" sensation slightly to the right of the umbilicus, with concomitant nausea and multiple episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He denies fever or chills. His last bowel movement was the same morning when symptoms started and was of normal appearance. Given his constellation of symptoms, he was instructed to present to for further workup. There, concern for recurrent small bowel obstruction was raised. An NGT was placed and imaging studies were obtained, with findings concerning for high-grade small bowel obstruction. Decision was made to transfer him to for definitive management. Of note, patient endorses relative control of his Crohn's disease. He has been on multiple drug regimens in the past (see for details), currently on prednisone 30 mg daily. Approximately once per week, he endorses episodes of abdominal pain and multiple loose bowel movements (and stool incontinence) that resolves after increasing the amount of prednisone taken from 30 to 40 mg that day. He states that he had one of those episodes the day prior to his presenting symptoms. His last colonoscopy was in revealing distal ileal ulcerations and disease, but no obstruction.
